[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/American_Hockey_League_alternate_logo.svg/330px-American_Hockey_League_alternate_logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:American_Hockey_League_alternate_logo.svg)

The alternate logo of the AHL

The **American Hockey League** (**AHL**) is a professional [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") league in [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America") that serves as the primary [developmental league](/wiki/Minor_league#Ice_hockey "Minor league") of the [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") (NHL).[[2]](#cite_note-2) The league comprises 32 teams, with 26 in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") and 6 in [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada").

As of the [2025–26 AHL season](/wiki/2025%E2%80%9326_AHL_season "2025–26 AHL season"), all 32 NHL teams held affiliations with an AHL team. Historically, when an NHL team does not have an AHL affiliate, its players are assigned to AHL teams affiliated with other NHL franchises. The league offices are located in [Springfield, Massachusetts](/wiki/Springfield,_Massachusetts "Springfield, Massachusetts"), and its current president is [Scott Howson](/wiki/Scott_Howson "Scott Howson").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

A player must be at least 18 years of age and not belong to a [junior ice hockey](/wiki/Junior_ice_hockey "Junior ice hockey") team to be eligible. The league limits the number of experienced professional players in a team's lineup during any given game; a team may dress a maximum of six skaters who are both 25 years old (or older) by July 1 of the current season and have accumulated more than 260 regular season games played across the National Hockey League, American Hockey League, [Kontinental Hockey League](/wiki/Kontinental_Hockey_League "Kontinental Hockey League"), or the six founding leagues of the [Champions Hockey League](/wiki/Champions_Hockey_League#Teams_and_format "Champions Hockey League"). Goaltenders are exempt from this rule, and games played in the European Elite Leagues before and during a player's final U-20 year will not count towards the Veteran Rule qualifications.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The annual playoff champion is awarded the [Calder Cup](/wiki/Calder_Cup "Calder Cup"), named for [Frank Calder](/wiki/Frank_Calder "Frank Calder"), the first president (1917–1943) of the NHL. The defending champions following the [2024–25 season](/wiki/2024%E2%80%9325_AHL_season "2024–25 AHL season") are the [Abbotsford Canucks](/wiki/Abbotsford_Canucks "Abbotsford Canucks"), winning their first Calder Cup in franchise history.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_Hockey_League&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Predecessor leagues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_Hockey_League&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Predecessor leagues")]

The AHL traces its origins directly to two predecessor professional leagues: the [Canadian-American Hockey League](/wiki/Canadian-American_Hockey_League "Canadian-American Hockey League") (the "Can-Am" League), founded in 1926, and the first [International Hockey League](/wiki/International_Hockey_League_(1929%E2%80%9336) "International Hockey League (1929–36)"), established in 1929. Although the Can-Am League never operated with more than six teams, the departure of the [Boston Bruin Cubs](/wiki/Boston_Tigers_(CAHL) "Boston Tigers (CAHL)") after the 1935–36 season reduced it down to just four member clubs: the [Springfield Indians](/wiki/Springfield_Indians "Springfield Indians"), [Philadelphia Ramblers](/wiki/Philadelphia_Ramblers "Philadelphia Ramblers"), [Providence Reds](/wiki/Providence_Reds "Providence Reds"), and [New Haven Eagles](/wiki/New_Haven_Eagles "New Haven Eagles") for the first time in its history. At the same time, the then-rival IHL lost half of its eight members after the 1935–36 season, leaving it with just four member teams: the [Buffalo Bisons](/wiki/Buffalo_Bisons_(IHL) "Buffalo Bisons (IHL)"), [Syracuse Stars](/wiki/Syracuse_Stars_(ice_hockey) "Syracuse Stars (ice hockey)"), [Pittsburgh Hornets](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Hornets "Pittsburgh Hornets"), and [Cleveland Falcons](/wiki/Cleveland_Falcons "Cleveland Falcons").

### 1936–1938

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_Hockey_League&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1936–1938")]

With both leagues down to the bare minimum number of teams to be viable, the governors of both leagues recognized the need for action to assure their member clubs' long-term survival. Their solution was to play an interlocking schedule. While the Can-Am was based in the [Northeast](/wiki/Northeastern_United_States "Northeastern United States") and the IHL in the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes_Region_(North_America) "Great Lakes Region (North America)"), their footprints were close enough for this to be a viable option. The two leagues' eight surviving clubs began joint play in November 1936 as a new two-division "circuit of mutual convenience" known as the International-American Hockey League. The four Can-Am teams became the I-AHL East Division, with its former trophy, the Henri Fontaine Cup, now going to the champion of the new East Division based on regular-season points. The IHL quartet played as the West Division. with its former championship trophy, the [F.G. "Teddy" Oke Trophy](/wiki/F.G._%22Teddy%22_Oke_Trophy "F.G. \"Teddy\" Oke Trophy"), going to the regular-season winners of the West Division. The Oke Trophy continues on, it is now awarded to the regular-season winners of the AHL's North Division, while the Fontaine Cup was retired in 1952, the year the AHL went to a single-division standing.

A little more than a month into that first season, the balance and symmetry of the new combined circuit suffered a setback when its membership unexpectedly fell to seven teams. The West's Buffalo Bisons were forced to cease operations on December 6, 1936, after playing just 11 games, because of what proved to be insurmountable financial problems and lack of access to a suitable arena; the Bisons' original arena, [Peace Bridge Arena](/wiki/Peace_Bridge_Arena "Peace Bridge Arena"), had collapsed the previous season (a new [Buffalo Bisons](/wiki/Buffalo_Bisons_(AHL) "Buffalo Bisons (AHL)") team would return to the league in 1940 after [a new arena](/wiki/Buffalo_Memorial_Auditorium "Buffalo Memorial Auditorium") was constructed for them). The makeshift new I-AHL played out the rest of its first season (as well as all of the next) with just seven teams.

At the end of the 1936–37 season, a modified three-round playoff format was devised and a new championship trophy, the [Calder Cup](/wiki/Calder_Cup "Calder Cup"), was established. The Syracuse Stars defeated the Philadelphia Ramblers in the final, three-games-to-one, to win the first-ever Calder Cup championship. The Calder Cup continues on today as the AHL's playoff championship trophy.

### Formal consolidation of the I-AHL

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_Hockey_League&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Formal consolidation of the I-AHL")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/a/ab/%22Hershey_in_Hockey_League%22_%28from_The_Philadelphia_Record%2C_6-29-1938%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:%22Hershey_in_Hockey_League%22_(from_The_Philadelphia_Record,_6-29-1938).jpg)

A June 29, 1938 [Associated Press](/wiki/Associated_Press "Associated Press") article in *[The Philadelphia Record](/wiki/The_Philadelphia_Record "The Philadelphia Record")* announcing the formation of the [Hershey Bears](/wiki/Hershey_Bears "Hershey Bears") in [Hershey, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Hershey,_Pennsylvania "Hershey, Pennsylvania")

After two seasons of interlocking play, the governors of the two leagues' seven active teams met in New York City on June 28, 1938, and agreed that it was time to formally consolidate. [Maurice Podoloff](/wiki/Maurice_Podoloff "Maurice Podoloff") of New Haven, the former head of the Can-Am League, was elected the I-AHL's first president. The former IHL president, [John D. Chick](/wiki/John_D._Chick "John D. Chick") of [Windsor, Ontario](/wiki/Windsor,_Ontario "Windsor, Ontario"), became vice-president in charge of officials.

The new I-AHL also added an eighth franchise at the 1938 meeting to fill the void in its membership left by the loss of Buffalo two years earlier with the admission of the then two-time defending [Eastern Amateur Hockey League](/wiki/Eastern_Hockey_League "Eastern Hockey League") (EAHL) champion [Hershey Bears](/wiki/Hershey_Bears "Hershey Bears").[[5]](#cite_note-5) The Bears remain the only one of these eight original I-AHL/AHL franchises to have been represented in the league without interruption since the 1938–39 season. The newly merged circuit also increased its regular-season schedule for each team by six games from 48 to 54.

### Contraction, resurrection, and expansion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_Hockey_League&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Contraction, resurrection, and expansion")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/AHL_50_Jahre.png/330px-AHL_50_Jahre.png)](/wiki/File:AHL_50_Jahre.png)

American Hockey League's 50th anniversary logo

After the 1939–40 season the I-AHL renamed itself the **American Hockey League**. It generally enjoyed both consistent success on the ice and relative financial stability over its first three decades of operation. In the late 1960s and early 1970s, however, the cost of doing business in professional ice hockey began to rise sharply with NHL expansion and relocation (the NHL placed teams in Pittsburgh and Buffalo, forcing two long-time AHL clubs, the Pittsburgh Hornets and Buffalo Bisons, to fold) and especially the 1972 formation of the [World Hockey Association](/wiki/World_Hockey_Association "World Hockey Association") (WHA), which forced the relocation and subsequent folding of the [Cleveland Barons](/wiki/Cleveland_Barons_(1937%E2%80%9373) "Cleveland Barons (1937–73)"), [Baltimore Clippers](/wiki/Baltimore_Clippers "Baltimore Clippers"), and [Quebec Aces](/wiki/Quebec_Aces "Quebec Aces"). The number of major-league teams competing for players rose from six to thirty in just seven years. Player salaries at all levels shot up dramatically with the increased demand and competition for their services.

This did not seem to affect the AHL at first, as it expanded to 12 teams by 1970. However, to help compensate for the rise in player salaries, many NHL clubs cut back on the number of players they kept under contract for development, and players under AHL contracts could now also demand much higher paychecks to remain with their clubs. As a result, half of the AHL's teams folded from 1974 to 1977. The league bottomed out in the summer of 1977, with news that the Rhode Island (formerly Providence) Reds – the last remaining uninterrupted franchise from the 1936–37 season, and the oldest continuously operating minor league franchise in North America – had decided to cease operations after 51 years in Rhode Island.

The AHL appeared in serious danger of folding altogether if this downward trend was not reversed. However, two events in the fall of 1977 helped reverse the trend. The first of these was the decision of the NHL's [Philadelphia Flyers](/wiki/Philadelphia_Flyers "Philadelphia Flyers") to return to the league as a team owner, and the second was the unexpected collapse of the [North American Hockey League](/wiki/North_American_Hockey_League_(1973%E2%80%931977) "North American Hockey League (1973–1977)") just weeks before the start of the 1977–78 season.

The Flyers' new AHL franchise became the immediately successful [Maine Mariners](/wiki/Maine_Mariners_(AHL) "Maine Mariners (AHL)"), which brought the new AHL city of [Portland, Maine](/wiki/Portland,_Maine "Portland, Maine") both the regular-season and Calder Cup playoff titles in each of that club's first two seasons of operation. The folding of the NAHL, meanwhile, suddenly left two of its stronger teams, the [Philadelphia Firebirds](/wiki/Philadelphia_Firebirds_(ice_hockey) "Philadelphia Firebirds (ice hockey)") and [Binghamton, New York](/wiki/Binghamton,_New_York "Binghamton, New York")-based [Broome Dusters](/wiki/Broome_Dusters "Broome Dusters"), without a league to play in. The owners of the Dusters solved their problem by buying the Reds franchise and moving it to Binghamton as the [Binghamton Dusters](/wiki/Binghamton_Dusters "Binghamton Dusters"), while the Firebirds crossed over to the AHL from the NAHL. The Dusters and Firebirds, together with the [Hampton Gulls](/wiki/Hampton_Gulls "Hampton Gulls") (who had joined the league from the [Southern Hockey League](/wiki/Southern_Hockey_League_(1973%E2%80%931977) "Southern Hockey League (1973–1977)")), boosted the AHL to nine member clubs as the 1977–78 season opened. Hampton folded on February 10, 1978, but was replaced the next year by the [New Brunswick Hawks](/wiki/New_Brunswick_Hawks "New Brunswick Hawks"). With franchise stability improving after the demise of the WHA in 1979, the league continued to grow steadily over the years, reaching 20 clubs by the 2000–01 season.

### Absorption of the IHL

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_Hockey_League&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Absorption of the IHL")]

In 2001–02, the AHL's membership jumped dramatically to 27 teams, mostly by the absorption of six teams—[Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee_Admirals "Milwaukee Admirals"), [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago_Wolves "Chicago Wolves"), [Houston](/wiki/Houston_Aeros_(1994%E2%80%932013) "Houston Aeros (1994–2013)"), [Utah](/wiki/Utah_Grizzlies_(1995%E2%80%932005) "Utah Grizzlies (1995–2005)"), [Manitoba](/wiki/Manitoba_Moose "Manitoba Moose"), and [Grand Rapids](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Griffins "Grand Rapids Griffins")—from the [International Hockey League](/wiki/International_Hockey_League "International Hockey League"). The IHL had established itself as the second top-level minor league circuit in North America, but folded in 2001 due to financial problems. One oddity caused by the AHL's 2001 expansion was that the league had two teams with the same nickname: the Milwaukee Admirals and the [Norfolk Admirals](/wiki/Norfolk_Admirals_(AHL) "Norfolk Admirals (AHL)"). The latter team transferred to the league from the mid-level [ECHL](/wiki/ECHL "ECHL") in 2000. This situation lasted until the end of the 2014–15 season when the Norfolk team moved to San Diego and was replaced by another ECHL team with the same name.

The Utah Grizzlies suspended operations after the 2004–05 season (the franchise was sold in 2006 and returned to the ice in Cleveland in [2007](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_AHL_season "2007–08 AHL season") as the Lake Erie Monsters, now known as the [Cleveland Monsters](/wiki/Cleveland_Monsters "Cleveland Monsters")). The Chicago Wolves ([2002](/wiki/2002_Calder_Cup_playoffs "2002 Calder Cup playoffs"), [2008](/wiki/2008_Calder_Cup_playoffs "2008 Calder Cup playoffs"), [2022](/wiki/2022_Calder_Cup_playoffs "2022 Calder Cup playoffs")), Houston Aeros ([2003](/wiki/2003_Calder_Cup_playoffs "2003 Calder Cup playoffs")), Milwaukee Admirals ([2004](/wiki/2004_Calder_Cup_playoffs "2004 Calder Cup playoffs")), and Grand Rapids Griffins ([2013](/wiki/2013_Calder_Cup_playoffs "2013 Calder Cup playoffs"), [2017](/wiki/2017_Calder_Cup_playoffs "2017 Calder Cup playoffs")) have all won Calder Cup titles since joining the AHL from the IHL. Chicago and Milwaukee have also made multiple trips to the Calder Cup Finals, and Houston made their second Finals appearance in 2011.

The Manitoba Moose moved to [St. John's, Newfoundland and Labrador](/wiki/St._John%27s,_Newfoundland_and_Labrador "St. John's, Newfoundland and Labrador") in 2011 and were renamed the [St. John's IceCaps](/wiki/St._John%27s_IceCaps "St. John's IceCaps") after the NHL's [Atlanta Thrashers](/wiki/Atlanta_Thrashers "Atlanta Thrashers") moved to Winnipeg as the second incarnation of the [Winnipeg Jets](/wiki/Winnipeg_Jets "Winnipeg Jets"). In 2013, Houston moved to Des Moines, Iowa to become the [Iowa Wild](/wiki/Iowa_Wild "Iowa Wild"). This left Chicago, Grand Rapids and Milwaukee as the only ex-IHL teams still in their original cities until the 2015 relocations when the IceCaps moved back to Winnipeg as the Manitoba Moose.

### Relocations and western shift

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_Hockey_League&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Relocations and western shift")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/American_Hockey_League_2014-15_map_zoomed.svg/500px-American_Hockey_League_2014-15_map_zoomed.svg.png)](/wiki/File:American_Hockey_League_2014-15_map_zoomed.svg)

Team locations and divisional alignment in the 2014–15 season prior to the franchise relocations

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/American_Hockey_League_2015-16_map_zoomed.svg/500px-American_Hockey_League_2015-16_map_zoomed.svg.png)](/wiki/File:American_Hockey_League_2015-16_map_zoomed.svg)

Team locations and divisions after the 2015–16 relocation and realignment

Beginning with the [2015–16 season](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9316_AHL_season "2015–16 AHL season"), twelve franchises have since relocated due to NHL parent clubs' influence on their development teams and players. Of the twelve relocated franchises, nine were relocated because they were directly owned by NHL teams and the NHL parent club wished to make call-ups from the AHL more practical by having closer affiliates.

In January 2015, the AHL announced the relocation of five existing AHL franchises—[Adirondack](/wiki/Adirondack_Flames "Adirondack Flames"), [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester_Monarchs_(AHL) "Manchester Monarchs (AHL)"), [Norfolk](/wiki/Norfolk_Admirals_(AHL) "Norfolk Admirals (AHL)"), [Oklahoma City](/wiki/Oklahoma_City_Barons "Oklahoma City Barons"), and [Worcester](/wiki/Worcester_Sharks "Worcester Sharks")—to California as the basis for a new "Pacific Division" becoming [Stockton](/wiki/Stockton_Heat "Stockton Heat"), [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario_Reign "Ontario Reign"), [San Diego](/wiki/San_Diego_Gulls "San Diego Gulls"), [Bakersfield](/wiki/Bakersfield_Condors "Bakersfield Condors"), and [San Jose](/wiki/San_Jose_Barracuda "San Jose Barracuda") respectively.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The relocated teams were all affiliated and owned or purchased by teams in the NHL's [Pacific Division](/wiki/Pacific_Division_(NHL) "Pacific Division (NHL)"). The franchise movements continued with two more relocations involving Canadian teams[[7]](#cite_note-7) with the [St. John's IceCaps](/wiki/St._John%27s_IceCaps "St. John's IceCaps") going back to [Winnipeg](/wiki/Winnipeg "Winnipeg") as the [Manitoba Moose](/wiki/Manitoba_Moose "Manitoba Moose") and the [Hamilton Bulldogs](/wiki/Hamilton_Bulldogs_(AHL) "Hamilton Bulldogs (AHL)") becoming another iteration of the IceCaps to fulfill the arena contract in St. John's.

In the following seasons, more NHL organizations influenced league membership. In 2016, the [Springfield Falcons](/wiki/Springfield_Falcons "Springfield Falcons") franchise was purchased by the [Arizona Coyotes](/wiki/Arizona_Coyotes "Arizona Coyotes") and relocated to become the [Tucson Roadrunners](/wiki/Tucson_Roadrunners "Tucson Roadrunners") and join the one-year-old Pacific Division. The Falcons were subsequently replaced by the [Springfield Thunderbirds](/wiki/Springfield_Thunderbirds "Springfield Thunderbirds"), the relocated [Portland Pirates](/wiki/Portland_Pirates "Portland Pirates") franchise under a new ownership group. The [Montreal Canadiens](/wiki/Montreal_Canadiens "Montreal Canadiens")-owned IceCaps relocated to the Montreal suburb of [Laval, Quebec](/wiki/Laval,_Quebec "Laval, Quebec"), and became the [Laval Rocket](/wiki/Laval_Rocket "Laval Rocket") in 2017.[[8]](#cite_note-The_Compass-8) The [Binghamton Senators](/wiki/Binghamton_Senators "Binghamton Senators") were also purchased by the [Ottawa Senators](/wiki/Ottawa_Senators "Ottawa Senators") and were relocated to [Belleville, Ontario](/wiki/Belleville,_Ontario "Belleville, Ontario"), to become the [Belleville Senators](/wiki/Belleville_Senators "Belleville Senators")[[9]](#cite_note-9) while the [New Jersey Devils](/wiki/New_Jersey_Devils "New Jersey Devils")' owned [Albany Devils](/wiki/Albany_Devils "Albany Devils") were relocated to become the [Binghamton Devils](/wiki/Binghamton_Devils "Binghamton Devils").[[10]](#cite_note-10)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Lehigh_Valley_Phantoms_Hartford_Wolf_Pack_December_14_2019_1_%28cropped%29.jpg/330px-Lehigh_Valley_Phantoms_Hartford_Wolf_Pack_December_14_2019_1_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lehigh_Valley_Phantoms_Hartford_Wolf_Pack_December_14_2019_1_(cropped).jpg)

The [Lehigh Valley Phantoms](/wiki/Lehigh_Valley_Phantoms "Lehigh Valley Phantoms") host the [Hartford Wolf Pack](/wiki/Hartford_Wolf_Pack "Hartford Wolf Pack") at [PPL Center](/wiki/PPL_Center "PPL Center") in [Allentown, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Allentown,_Pennsylvania "Allentown, Pennsylvania"), December 2019

For the [2018–19 season](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_AHL_season "2018–19 AHL season"), a 31st team joined the league with the [Colorado Eagles](/wiki/Colorado_Eagles "Colorado Eagles") as the NHL's [Colorado Avalanche](/wiki/Colorado_Avalanche "Colorado Avalanche") affiliate.[[11]](#cite_note-11) With the NHL planning to expand to 32 teams in 2021 with the [Seattle Kraken](/wiki/Seattle_Kraken "Seattle Kraken"), the Seattle ownership group was approved for a 2021 AHL expansion team, later announced to be the [Coachella Valley Firebirds](/wiki/Coachella_Valley_Firebirds "Coachella Valley Firebirds") based in [Palm Desert, California](/wiki/Palm_Desert,_California "Palm Desert, California"), following the construction of [a new arena](/wiki/Coachella_Valley_Arena "Coachella Valley Arena").[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) The original plans for the new arena was eventually cancelled and the team postponed their launch by a year while new arena plans were developed.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

In February 2020, the [San Antonio Rampage](/wiki/San_Antonio_Rampage "San Antonio Rampage") franchise was bought and relocated by the NHL's [Vegas Golden Knights](/wiki/Vegas_Golden_Knights "Vegas Golden Knights") for the [2020–21 season](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_AHL_season "2020–21 AHL season")[[15]](#cite_note-15) as the [Henderson Silver Knights](/wiki/Henderson_Silver_Knights "Henderson Silver Knights") and was moved to the Pacific Division. For the [2021–22 season](/wiki/2021%E2%80%9322_AHL_season "2021–22 AHL season"), the [Vancouver Canucks](/wiki/Vancouver_Canucks "Vancouver Canucks") relocated their franchise from Utica to Abbotsford while the [Utica Comets](/wiki/Utica_Comets "Utica Comets") agreed to relocate and operate the franchise that was operating as the [Binghamton Devils](/wiki/Binghamton_Devils "Binghamton Devils").[[16]](#cite_note-16) On May 23, 2022, it was announced that the Stockton Heat would be relocating to [Calgary](/wiki/Calgary "Calgary"), [Alberta](/wiki/Alberta "Alberta"), starting the [2022–23 season](/wiki/2022%E2%80%9323_AHL_season "2022–23 AHL season").[[17]](#cite_note-17)

For the [2023–24 season](/wiki/2023%E2%80%9324_AHL_season "2023–24 AHL season"), the Chicago Wolves operated as the league's only unaffiliated team, making them the first team to operate without an NHL partner since the inaugural season of the [Worcester IceCats](/wiki/Worcester_IceCats "Worcester IceCats") during the [1994–95 season](/wiki/1994%E2%80%9395_AHL_season "1994–95 AHL season").[[18]](#cite_note-UPICats-18) Consequently, the [Carolina Hurricanes](/wiki/Carolina_Hurricanes "Carolina Hurricanes") became the only NHL team currently without an AHL affiliate.[[19]](#cite_note-19) However, the Hurricanes loaned some players to the Wolves, such as [Vasili Ponomaryov](/wiki/Vasili_Ponomaryov "Vasili Ponomaryov"), Domenick Fensore, Ronan Seeley and [Antti Raanta](/wiki/Antti_Raanta "Antti Raanta"). On May 2, 2024, the Hurricanes and Wolves committed to a three-year affiliation beginning with the 2024–25 season.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

## All-Star Game

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_Hockey_League&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: All-Star Game")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/BWP_6505_%2826105839808%29.jpg/250px-BWP_6505_%2826105839808%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:BWP_6505_(26105839808).jpg)

Cheerleader cheering at the All-Star Game, 2018

The American Hockey League first held an All-Star Game in the 1941–42 season as a fundraiser for [American Red Cross](/wiki/American_Red_Cross "American Red Cross") and [Canadian Red Cross](/wiki/Canadian_Red_Cross "Canadian Red Cross") efforts during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). Players from the Eastern Division faced off against players from the Western Division at Cleveland Arena.[[22]](#cite_note-23)

The event was not played again until the 1954–55 season, and was then held annually until the 1959–60 season. These six annual games pitted a team of all-stars against the defending Calder Cup champions (with the exception of the 1959-60 event, which featured the Springfield Indians).

The modern AHL All-Star Game was reinstituted for the 1994–95 season and a skills competition was introduced in 1995–96, with the two-day event being dubbed the AHL All-Star Classic. The 1995 and 1996 games featured players from teams based in Canada taking on players from teams based in the United States. Beginning in 1997, Canadian-born players faced players born outside Canada (known as the "World" team in 1997 and "PlanetUSA" from 1998 to 2010).

The format was revamped again in 2011 to pit the Eastern Conference against the Western Conference. In 2014, a team of AHL all-stars hosted the [Swedish Hockey League](/wiki/Swedish_Hockey_League "Swedish Hockey League") club [Färjestad BK](/wiki/F%C3%A4rjestad_BK "Färjestad BK").

Since 2016, the all-star game has been replaced by the AHL All-Star Challenge,[[23]](#cite_note-24) a three-on-three round-robin tournament among teams from the league's four divisions; the top two teams advance to the final game, with the winner declared the challenge champions.

The AHL All-Star Classic was postponed in 2021 and 2022 due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"). The [Laval Rocket](/wiki/Laval_Rocket "Laval Rocket"), who had been selected to host, ultimately hosted the event in 2023.

## Outdoor games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_Hockey_League&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Outdoor games")]

Main article: [AHL Outdoor Classic](/wiki/AHL_Outdoor_Classic "AHL Outdoor Classic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/2012_AHL_Winter_Classic_CBP_Philadelphia.jpg/330px-2012_AHL_Winter_Classic_CBP_Philadelphia.jpg)](/wiki/File:2012_AHL_Winter_Classic_CBP_Philadelphia.jpg)

An AHL record crowd of 45,653 watched the [Adirondack Phantoms](/wiki/Adirondack_Phantoms "Adirondack Phantoms") defeat the [Hershey Bears](/wiki/Hershey_Bears "Hershey Bears"), 4–3 in overtime at the 2012 AHL Winter Classic at [Citizens Bank Park](/wiki/Citizens_Bank_Park "Citizens Bank Park") in [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia")

Since the 2009–10 season, at least one team in the AHL has hosted an [outdoor ice hockey game](/wiki/List_of_outdoor_ice_hockey_games "List of outdoor ice hockey games") each year. The [Syracuse Crunch](/wiki/Syracuse_Crunch "Syracuse Crunch") was the first organization to put on an outdoor game in the AHL on February 20, 2010, building a rink at the New York State Fairgrounds in [Syracuse, New York](/wiki/Syracuse,_New_York "Syracuse, New York"), and packing a record 21,508 fans in for the [Mirabito Outdoor Classic](/wiki/Mirabito_Outdoor_Classic "Mirabito Outdoor Classic") against the [Binghamton Senators](/wiki/Binghamton_Senators "Binghamton Senators"). The contest, which was also televised to an international audience on [NHL Network](/wiki/NHL_Network_(U.S._TV_network) "NHL Network (U.S. TV network)"), was won by the Crunch, 2–1.

The Connecticut Whale hosted the [Whale Bowl](/wiki/Whale_Bowl "Whale Bowl"), the AHL's second outdoor game held on February 19, 2011, as part of a 10-day Whalers Hockey Fest at [Rentschler Field](/wiki/Rentschler_Field_(stadium) "Rentschler Field (stadium)") in [East Hartford, Connecticut](/wiki/East_Hartford,_Connecticut "East Hartford, Connecticut"). Attendance for Connecticut's game against the [Providence Bruins](/wiki/Providence_Bruins "Providence Bruins") was announced at 21,673, the largest in AHL history to that point. Providence won, 5–4, in a shootout.

On January 6, 2012, the largest crowd in AHL history saw the [Adirondack Phantoms](/wiki/Adirondack_Phantoms "Adirondack Phantoms") defeat the [Hershey Bears](/wiki/Hershey_Bears "Hershey Bears"), 4–3, in overtime before 45,653 fans at [Citizens Bank Park](/wiki/Citizens_Bank_Park "Citizens Bank Park") in [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"), as the final event of the week-long activities associated with the [2012 NHL Winter Classic](/wiki/2012_NHL_Winter_Classic "2012 NHL Winter Classic"), which also included a game between the Philadelphia Flyers and the [New York Rangers](/wiki/New_York_Rangers "New York Rangers") on Jan 2 and an alumni game between retired players (including eight honored members of the [Hockey Hall of Fame](/wiki/Hockey_Hall_of_Fame "Hockey Hall of Fame")) of those two clubs on December 31, 2011. The contest was the third outdoor game in AHL history and it more than doubled the league's previous single-game attendance mark.

On January 21, 2012, the Steeltown Showdown between Ontario rivals, the [Toronto Marlies](/wiki/Toronto_Marlies "Toronto Marlies") and [Hamilton Bulldogs](/wiki/Hamilton_Bulldogs_(AHL) "Hamilton Bulldogs (AHL)"), was held at [Ivor Wynne Stadium](/wiki/Ivor_Wynne_Stadium "Ivor Wynne Stadium") in [Hamilton, Ontario](/wiki/Hamilton,_Ontario "Hamilton, Ontario"), with the Marlies winning 7–2 in front of 20,565 fans, the largest crowd ever for an AHL game in Canada. The AHL game was preceded the previous night by a game between [Toronto Maple Leafs](/wiki/Toronto_Maple_Leafs "Toronto Maple Leafs") and [Montreal Canadiens](/wiki/Montreal_Canadiens "Montreal Canadiens") alumni.

Two outdoor games were announced for the 2012–13 AHL season, but a meeting between the [Grand Rapids Griffins](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Griffins "Grand Rapids Griffins") and Toronto Marlies at [Comerica Park](/wiki/Comerica_Park "Comerica Park") in [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit") as part of the festivities surrounding the [NHL Winter Classic](/wiki/2013_NHL_Winter_Classic "2013 NHL Winter Classic") was not held because of the cancellation of the NHL Winter Classic. On January 20, 2013, the Hershey Bears and [Wilkes-Barre/Scranton Penguins](/wiki/Wilkes-Barre/Scranton_Penguins "Wilkes-Barre/Scranton Penguins") met outdoors at [Hersheypark Stadium](/wiki/Hersheypark_Stadium "Hersheypark Stadium") in [Hershey, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Hershey,_Pennsylvania "Hershey, Pennsylvania") with the Penguins earning a 2–1 overtime victory in front of 17,311 fans.

The [Rochester Americans](/wiki/Rochester_Americans "Rochester Americans") hosted an outdoor game in 2013–14, the [Frozen Frontier](/wiki/Frozen_Frontier "Frozen Frontier"), which was held at [Frontier Field](/wiki/Frontier_Field "Frontier Field") in [Rochester, New York](/wiki/Rochester,_New_York "Rochester, New York"), on December 13, 2013. The Americans took a 5–4 decision in a shootout against the [Lake Erie Monsters](/wiki/Lake_Erie_Monsters "Lake Erie Monsters") before a standing-room crowd of 11,015 fans. A year after their originally scheduled date, the Griffins and Marlies played at Comerica Park on December 30, 2013, and Toronto prevailed in a shootout, 4–3, becoming the first AHL team ever with two outdoor wins. Attendance in Detroit was 20,337.

As part of the recent addition of the Pacific Division, the AHL played its first outdoor hockey game in California during the 2015–16 season, called the Golden State Hockey Rush. On December 18, 2015, the [Stockton Heat](/wiki/Stockton_Heat "Stockton Heat") defeated the [Bakersfield Condors](/wiki/Bakersfield_Condors "Bakersfield Condors"), 3–2, at [Raley Field](/wiki/Raley_Field "Raley Field") in [West Sacramento, California](/wiki/West_Sacramento,_California "West Sacramento, California").[[30]](#cite_note-31)

For the second consecutive season, the AHL played an outdoor game in California as the Bakersfield Condors hosted the Condorstown Outdoor Classic against the [Ontario Reign](/wiki/Ontario_Reign "Ontario Reign") on January 7, 2017, at [Bakersfield College](/wiki/Bakersfield_College "Bakersfield College")'s [Memorial Stadium](/wiki/Memorial_Stadium_(Bakersfield) "Memorial Stadium (Bakersfield)").[[31]](#cite_note-32) Despite sometimes heavy rain during the first period, the game went on as scheduled and the Condors defeated the Reign 3–2 in overtime.

The 2021 AIS Empire State Outdoor Classic was scheduled for February 13th 2021 and to be played at Griffiss Business & Technology Park in Rome, NY between the Syracuse Crunch and Utica Comets but was canceled in 2020 due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").[[32]](#cite_note-33)

Hersheypark Stadium hosted its second outdoor game in 2018. Cleveland's [FirstEnergy Stadium](/wiki/FirstEnergy_Stadium "FirstEnergy Stadium") became the first [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") venue to host an AHL outdoor game in 2023, and [Truist Field](/wiki/Truist_Field "Truist Field") in Charlotte hosted the Queen City Outdoor Classic in 2024.

On January 23, 2026, the Iowa Wild Outdoor Classic was held as part of [Hockey Day Minnesota](/wiki/Hockey_Day_Minnesota "Hockey Day Minnesota") in Hastings, Minn., with the [Milwaukee Admirals](/wiki/Milwaukee_Admirals "Milwaukee Admirals") defeating the [Iowa Wild](/wiki/Iowa_Wild "Iowa Wild"), 3-2 in overtime. With a game-time temperature of minus-8 degrees Fahrenheit (minus-22 Celsius), it was the coldest outdoor game in North American history.[[33]](#cite_note-34)

## International games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_Hockey_League&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: International games")]

Teams from the AHL have competed against non-North American teams, in both international tournaments and one-off matchups.

Several club teams from the Soviet Union toured the United States and Canada during the 1970s and 1980s and played exhibitions against AHL clubs, including the [Soviet Wings](/wiki/Krylya_Sovetov_Moscow "Krylya Sovetov Moscow") (1974–75), [HC Spartak Moscow](/wiki/HC_Spartak_Moscow "HC Spartak Moscow") (1976, 1986), [Moscow Khimik](/wiki/Atlant_Moscow_Oblast "Atlant Moscow Oblast") (1976), [HC Dynamo Moscow](/wiki/HC_Dynamo_Moscow "HC Dynamo Moscow") (1977–78), [Traktor Chelyabinsk](/wiki/Traktor_Chelyabinsk "Traktor Chelyabinsk") (1978–79) and [Sokil Kyiv](/wiki/Sokil_Kyiv "Sokil Kyiv") (1989).

Prior to the [1980 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1980_Winter_Olympics "1980 Winter Olympics") in Lake Placid, New York, the [Adirondack Red Wings](/wiki/Adirondack_Red_Wings "Adirondack Red Wings") hosted exhibition games against the national teams from the [United States](/wiki/United_States_at_the_1980_Winter_Olympics "United States at the 1980 Winter Olympics"), [West Germany](/wiki/West_Germany_at_the_1980_Winter_Olympics "West Germany at the 1980 Winter Olympics"), [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden_at_the_1980_Winter_Olympics "Sweden at the 1980 Winter Olympics") and [Finland](/wiki/Finland_at_the_1980_Winter_Olympics "Finland at the 1980 Winter Olympics"). Team USA also played exhibitions against AHL teams leading up to the [1994 Olympics](/wiki/1994_Olympics "1994 Olympics") in Lillehammer, Norway.

The [Rochester Americans](/wiki/Rochester_Americans "Rochester Americans") participated in the [1996](/wiki/1996_Spengler_Cup "1996 Spengler Cup") and [2013](/wiki/2013_Spengler_Cup "2013 Spengler Cup") editions of the [Spengler Cup](/wiki/Spengler_Cup "Spengler Cup"), held in [Davos](/wiki/Davos "Davos"), [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland"). The Spengler Cup is an annual invitational tournament featuring teams from leagues around Europe and the world. The [participation of the AHL](/wiki/AHL_participation_at_the_Spengler_Cup "AHL participation at the Spengler Cup") in future Spengler Cups has been discussed by both the tournament organizers and league leadership.[[34]](#cite_note-35)

In 2014, Swedish club [Färjestad BK](/wiki/F%C3%A4rjestad_BK "Färjestad BK") met the [Toronto Marlies](/wiki/Toronto_Marlies "Toronto Marlies") as part of a visit to Canada that included its participation in the 2014 AHL All-Star Classic.[[35]](#cite_note-36)

In February 2018, the [Ontario Reign](/wiki/Ontario_Reign "Ontario Reign") hosted and defeated the [DEL's](/wiki/Deutsche_Eishockey_Liga "Deutsche Eishockey Liga") [Eisbären Berlin](/wiki/Eisb%C3%A4ren_Berlin "Eisbären Berlin"), 6–3, in a friendly matchup organized by [Anschutz Entertainment Group](/wiki/Anschutz_Entertainment_Group "Anschutz Entertainment Group"), the owner of both teams.[[36]](#cite_note-37)

## AHL Hall of Fame

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=American_Hockey_League&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: AHL Hall of Fame")]

Main article: [AHL Hall of Fame](/wiki/AHL_Hall_of_Fame "AHL Hall of Fame")

The formation of an American Hockey League Hall of Fame was announced by the league on December 15, 2005, created to recognize, honor and celebrate individuals for their outstanding achievements and contributions specifically in the AHL.[[37]](#cite_note-38)

* [Fred T. Hunt Memorial Award](/wiki/Fred_T._Hunt_Memorial_Award "Fred T. Hunt Memorial Award") – Sportsmanship / Perseverance *([1977–78](/wiki/1977%E2%80%9378_AHL_season "1977–78 AHL season"))*
